_pass: Level 3 Pass 1 + Rounds 1-3 visual polish + worked examples v2 + def promotion + LA trim + apps + extra exercises
title: What is a function?
deck: A function is one of the simplest, most useful ideas in mathematics, and we have been using functions our whole lives without knowing the name.
objectives: Define a function, write it in f(x) notation, evaluate it at a given input, and name its domain and range.

content.text: Imagine working a shift at $15 per hour. After six hours, your phone tells you that you have earned $90. After nine hours, it shows $135. You did not pull out a calculator. You knew the rule: hours in, dollars out. Multiply the number of hours by fifteen, and the result is what you have earned.

That rule, hours in and dollars out, is what mathematicians call a function. By the end of this section we will know how to write such a rule down, how to read it back, and how to use it to predict any value we might want.
1.1.1  The big idea: one rule, one output
A function is a rule that takes an input and produces exactly one output. Whatever input we give it today, it produces the same output tomorrow. That predictability is what makes a function useful.
Same input today, same input tomorrow, same answer every time.

Figure 1.1 captures the idea visually. An input x enters the function f, the rule transforms it, and one (and only one) output emerges. We can give the rule any name we like, but the traditional choice in mathematics is the letter f. Other common names are g and h.

content.caption: Figure 1.1. An input enters the function, the rule transforms it, and one (and only one) output emerges.

content.title: Function
content.content:
A **function** is a rule that assigns to each input exactly one output. Two pieces of vocabulary travel with the idea: the **input** is the value we feed in, almost always called *x*; the **output** is what comes back, often called *y* or *f(x)*.

Functions can be written in several ways — as a formula (y = 15x), a table of values, a graph, or a verbal description. Each form represents the same underlying rule.

content.text: Margin noteSome texts use g(x) or h(x) when several functions appear on the same page, to avoid ambiguity with f(x). The letter is just a name; the math is identical.
1.1.2  Notation: f(x), inputs, and outputs

The standard way to write a function in mathematical notation is f(x). We read this aloud as “f of x,” and it means: the output of the function f when the input is x. It does not mean f times x. The parentheses are not a multiplication sign; they are a shorthand for “apply the function to.”
Common mistakeReading f(x) as f · x. The parentheses are not multiplication. Writing 2f(3) means “twice the value of f at 3,” not “two times f times 3.”

We have several names for the same pieces. The input is the value we feed in, almost always called x. The output is what comes back, often called y when written by itself, or f(x) when written together with the function. So in a rule like
y = f(x) = 15x
the x on the right side is whatever number we feed in, and the y on the left is what comes out.
1.1.3  Evaluating a function

To evaluate a function at a specific value means to compute its output for that input. The phrase “evaluate f at x = 7” means: substitute 7 wherever x appears in the rule, then simplify. For f(x) = 15x, the value of f(7) is
f(7) = 15 · 7 = 105
This is the move we will use again and again throughout the course. Whenever we see f(something), we replace x with that something and do the arithmetic.

Example 1.1. Your phone bill, written as a function.

A cell phone plan charges a flat $25 a month for the base fee, plus $0.10 for every minute the user goes over their data allowance. The monthly cost depends on the overage minutes. We want to write a function for the bill, then evaluate it at 0, 50, and 200 overage minutes.

Solution.

The bill consists of two pieces: a fixed part (the $25 base) and a variable part ($0.10 times however many overage minutes). Letting x be the overage minutes,

f(x) = 25 + 0.10x

Evaluating at the three inputs:

x25 + 0.10xf(x)
025 + 0.10(0)$25.00
5025 + 0.10(50)$30.00
20025 + 0.10(200)$45.00

Notice that the function never produces two answers for the same input. Fifty overage minutes always produces $30. That is the “exactly one output” property in action.

In the wildMost “fixed fee plus per-unit cost” billing schemes are functions of this same shape. Electricity bills, ride-share fares with a base charge, and many gym memberships all reduce to f(x) = a + bx, where a is the fixed part and b is the rate.
1.1.4  Domain and range

Not every input makes sense for every function. The domain of a function is the set of inputs that the rule actually accepts. For the phone-bill function, the domain is “any number of overage minutes from zero on up.” Working negative minutes does not make sense. The range is the set of outputs the function can actually produce. For the phone-bill function, the range is “any dollar amount from $25 on up.”

In later sections, where the rules get richer, the domain often comes with restrictions: square roots cannot accept negative inputs, division cannot accept zero in the denominator, and so on. For now we just need the names. The domain is the universe of legal inputs. The range is the universe of possible outputs.
Exercises

content.problem: A delivery app charges a flat $2.99 service fee plus $0.40 per mile. Write the cost function **C(m)** where *m* is the number of miles, then evaluate **C(7)**.
A fitness tracker estimates calories burned during a walk as **C(s) = 4s**, where *s* is the number of minutes walked. How many calories would a 25-minute walk burn according to the tracker?

content.text:
1. A coffee shop charges $4.50 per latte. Let x be the number of lattes purchased. Write the function f(x) = 4.50x, then evaluate f(7).

    Show answer

    Answer. f(7) = 4.50 · 7 = $31.50.

  2. A gym charges $30 a month, plus $2 per fitness class attended. With the function f(x) = 30 + 2x, what is the bill for a month with 12 classes?

    Show answer

    Answer. f(12) = 30 + 2(12) = 30 + 24 = $54.

  3. A function f is described by the table below. The rule is not shown. Predict f(4).

    xf(x)
    15
    28
    311
    4?
    Show answer

    Answer. The values increase by 3 with each step, so the rule is f(x) = 3x + 2, which gives f(4) = 14.
More worked examples

content.title: Evaluating a function
content.solution:
**Evaluate by substitution.** Wherever the rule has an *x*, put the input.

f(4) = 3(4) − 5 = 12 − 5 = **7**

f(−2) = 3(−2) − 5 = −6 − 5 = **−11**

Same rule, different inputs, different outputs. Once the rule is known, every input has exactly one output.

content.title: Is it a function?
content.problem: Decide whether the following set of (input, output) pairs defines a function: **{(1, 2), (2, 5), (1, 7), (3, 8)}**.
content.solution:
**Check the definition.** A function pairs each input with *exactly one* output.

Look at the inputs in the set: 1, 2, 1, 3. The input **1** appears twice — once with output 2, once with output 7. The same input cannot give two different outputs in a function.

So this set is **not a function**.

*The trap to notice.* The repetition has to be in the inputs, not the outputs. The set {(1, 5), (2, 5), (3, 5)} *is* a function — every input has one output, even though all the outputs happen to be the same.

content.title: Summary
content.content: A function is a rule that assigns to every input **exactly one** output. Evaluating a function means substituting a value for the variable; deciding whether a relation is a function means checking that no input is paired with two different outputs.

content.title: Functions in your phone
content.content: Open any rideshare app and watch the fare update as you change the drop-off pin. What you are watching is a function in action: the **input** is your destination (time + distance), the **output** is the fare in dollars. The same is true of DoorDash's delivery time estimate, Spotify's daily-mix algorithm, and the calorie counter on your fitness app. Each one is a *rule* that takes inputs and returns exactly one output. The math you'll learn in this chapter is the language those rules are written in — and once you can read a function, you can spot which apps are doing what (and where they might be wrong).

content.text: Looking ahead
In Problem 3 we guessed a rule from examples, working from specific cases to a general pattern.
